coredata.dc:title: Synthesis of multiple-color emitting carbon dots by co-doping of sulfur and nitrogen
coredata.dc:creator: Long, Xiang
Wang, Chenglin
Ma, Yanshun
Wu, Shaogui
Hao, Liang
coredata.dc:description: Herein, multiple color (blue, green and red) emissive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from the main precursor via different protocols, with emission wavelengths of 439 nm, 528 nm and 623 nm, respectively, realizing the synthesis of fluorescent CDs spanning the fluorescence emission from short to long wavelengths. Their relative PLQYs reach up to 28.9%, 31.4% and 12.3%, respectively, exceeding the PLQYs of many similar CDs. The green CDs (G-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs) show optimal photoluminescence performance in acetone (Me2CO); while the B-CDs have a pH-dependent response in the pH 2 to 12 range. All the three CDs have excellent photostability and salt resistance. Finally, serving as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink was taken as an example to briefly introduce the application of multi-color CDs. This work provides a new strategy to develop low-cost multiple color phosphors for display device, biological imaging, analysis and detection and other fields.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Solvothermal synthesis
Quantum yield
Multi-color

xocs:meta.xocs:articleinfo: articleinfo articlenumber articletitlenorm authfirstinitialnorm authfirstsurnamenorm cid cids contenttype copyright crossmark dateloaded dateloadedtxt datesearch datesort dateupdated dco docsubtype doctype doi eid ewtransactionid hubeid indexeddate issn issnnorm itemstage itemtransactionid itemweight openaccess openarchive pg pgfirst pii piinorm pubdateend pubdatestart pubdatetxt pubyr sectiontitle sortorder srctitle srctitlenorm srctype ssids alllist content subj subheadings suppl tomb volfirst volissue volumelist webpdf webpdfpagecount yearnav figure e-component body mmlmath acknowledge affil appendices articletitle auth authfirstini authfull authkeywords authlast grantnumber grantsponsor grantsponsorid highlightsabst orcid primabst ref

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic diagram of synthesis of multicolor CDs with ANSA as the main precursor.

Structural characterization of the three CDs. (a–c) Representive TEM images of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively. Insets: High resolution TEM images. (d) XRD patterns of the three CDs; (e) FT-IR spectra; (f) wide scan XPS spectra; (g–i) High-resolution C1s spectra of B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively.

Optical characteristics of the three CDs. (a–c) three-dimensional fluorescence spectra, (d–f) two-dimensional projection, (g–i) UV–Vis absorption spectra (purple), excitation spectra (blue), emission spectra (multi-color).

Dependence of fluorescence emission on excitation wavelength. (a–c) Emission spectra of blue, green, and red CDs at different excitation wavelengths and (d–f) normalized emission spectra at different excitation wavelengths.
PLQY of the three CDs in different solvents. (a-c) Integral fluorescence intensity vs UV–vis absorbance. (d-f) PLQYs.
Other optical properties. PL intensities of the three CDs as funcitons of EtOH:H2O volume ratio (a), concentration of NaCl (b), pH (c), and time (d).

Application of CDs as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink. The painting and handwriting are (A) invisible under sunlight, (B) visible under UV light. Note: hair and branches are drawn ordinary black ink, visible in daylight.

head.ce:title: Synthesis of multiple-color emitting carbon dots by co-doping of sulfur and nitrogen

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu, 610066, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Materials Science

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Materials Science, Sichuan Normal University, Chengdu 610066, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing, 100084, China
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemical Engineering, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein, multiple color (blue, green and red) emissive carbon dots (CDs) were synthesized from the main precursor via different protocols, with emission wavelengths of 439 nm, 528 nm and 623 nm, respectively, realizing the synthesis of fluorescent CDs spanning the fluorescence emission from short to long wavelengths. Their relative PLQYs reach up to 28.9%, 31.4% and 12.3%, respectively, exceeding the PLQYs of many similar CDs. The green CDs (G-CDs) and red CDs (R-CDs) show optimal photoluminescence performance in acetone (Me2CO); while the B-CDs have a pH-dependent response in the pH 2 to 12 range. All the three CDs have excellent photostability and salt resistance. Finally, serving as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink was taken as an example to briefly introduce the application of multi-color CDs. This work provides a new strategy to develop low-cost multiple color phosphors for display device, biological imaging, analysis and detection and other fields.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Carbon dots (CDs), as a class of promising carbon nano-fluorescent materials [1,2] with low toxicity, good biocompatibility, excellent photostability [3–6], exhibit potential applications in display equipment [7–9], energy conversion [10–12], bioimaging [13–15], chemical detection [16–20], fluorescent ink [21]. Therefore, CDs attract increasing interest since their first discovery in 2004 [22]. CDs synthesized in the early stage were mostly concentrated in the short-wavelength emission region, while the CDs with long-wavelength emission were few. In recent years, more and more orange or red fluorescent CDs have been synthesized with fairly high photoluminescence quantum yields (PLQYs). For example, Yuan et al. used resorcinol as a precursor to synthesize red-emitting CDs with an emission wavelength of 610 nm with a PLQY of 72% [23]. Jia et al. used N,N-dipropyl-P-PD as precursor to synthesize red CDs with an emission wavelength of 645 nm, and the PLQY was up to 86% [24]. In addition, the synthesis scale has also been significantly improved. Niu et al. used phloroglucinol and boric acid as precursors to synthesize red CDs (620 nm) with a high product yield by solid-phase synthesis, realizing the mass production of fluorescent CDs powder [25].

In recent years, multi-color emissive CDs are highly desired owing to their potential applications in various fields [26]. Xu et al. synthesized trichromatic CDs with dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA) as the main precursor with PLQY of 4.05%, 20.77%, and 1.76%, respectively [27]. However, their poor PLQYs limit their practical applications. Miao et al. synthesized tunable color emissive CDs by regulating the molar ratio of Citric acid/urea [28]. However, the synthesis is time consuming and the post-processing is complicated, which is not conducive to large-scale production.

In this work, we attempted to synthesize multi-color emissive CDs from same main precursor. It is reported that the optical properties and PLQY of CDs doped with heteroatoms can usually be further improved [29,30]. Among them, N and S are common doped atoms, which are widely used. The introduction of heteroatoms can change the electron density of adjacent groups, thereby improving the optical properties of CDs. Therefore, in this work, the precursor containing N and S heteroatoms is used to synthesize CDs. Since the synthesis of CDs of different colors requires different raw materials, some compounds with special structures, increase the cost and complexity of the synthesis of CDs. In this work, commercially available 1-amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid is used as the common precursor, and blue, green and red fluorescent CDs are synthesized by solvothermal method, realizing the full coverage of fluorescence emissions from short to long wavelengths. This work is of great significance for reducing the production cost and complexity of CDs, shedding light on developing low-cost multiple color phosphors, and boost their applications.

1-Amino-2-naphthol-4-sulfonic acid (ANSA, analytical grade, Shanghai Zhanyun Chemical Co., Ltd.), ethylenediamine (EDA, analytical grade, Shanghai Aladdin Biotechnology Co., Ltd.). Quinine sulfate was purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Experimental H2O was self-made double distilled H2O.

Hydrothermal reactor (25 mL) was purchased from Yanniu Technology Co., Ltd. Ultrasonic cleaning machine was purchased from Shanghai Fuyang Instrument Co., Ltd. Electric heating constant temperature blast drying oven was purchased from Shanghai Lichen Technology Co., Ltd. UV–vis spectra were measured by Sunny Hengping 756 PC UV–Vis spectrophotometer, and fluorescence spectra were obtained by Prism F98 fluorescence spectrophotometer.

ANSA (20 mg, 0.0836 mmol), urea (100 mg, 1.665 mmol) and KIO4 (80 mg, 0.3478 mmol) were accurately weight and dissolved in DMF (10 mL), and sonicated for 10 min. The solution was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor, heated at 180 °C for 8 h and cooled naturally. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm min−1 for 10 min to remove insoluble impurities. Then the mixture of MeOH-dichloromethane (volume ratio of 1:25) was used as the eluent to separate the supernatant to obtain B-CDs.

ANSA (20 mg, 0.0836 mmol), EDA (100 mg, 1.665 mmol) were dissolved in EtOH (10 mL), and sonicated for 10 min. The solution was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor, heated at 180 °C for 8 h and cooled naturally. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm min−1 for 10 min to eliminate insoluble impurities. The supernatant was collected and filtered with 0.22 μm microporous acetate fiber filter paper to obtain a clear solution, which was further dialyzed with dialysis bag (retained molecular weight 200–500) for 48 h to obtain G-CDs.

ANSA (20 mg, 0.0836 mmol), KIO4 (80 mg, 0.3478 mmol) were dissolved in EtOH (10 mL), and sonicated for 10 min. The solution was transferred to a 25 mL polytetrafluoroethylene hydrothermal reactor, heated at 180 °C for 8 h and cooled naturally. The reaction solution was centrifuged at 8000 rpm min−1 for 10 min to remove insoluble impurities. Then EtOH was used as the eluent to separate the supernatant to obtain R-CDs. The schematic diagram of synthesis of the multicolor CDs is illustrated in Fig. 1 .

Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR): CDs solution was placed in a vacuum oven and fully dried at 60 °C. The obtained CDs powder was mixed with KBr in the ratio of 1:100, grinded, pressed, and then FT-IR measurement was carried out with scanning range 4000 cm−1-400 cm−1.
X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS): The surface composition of dried CDs was determined by a Geneis 60 s X-ray photoelectron spectrometer with C1s (284.6 eV) as the internal standard.

Quinine sulfate was used as a standard reference to estimate the PLQY of CDs. According to the literature [31], quinine sulfate dissolved in 0.1 M sulfuric acid solution has a PLQY value of 0.54 at 360 nm. Then the relative PLQY of test sample could be estimated by the following equation,
(1)
PLQY U
=
PLQY S
⋅
F U
F S
⋅
A S
A U
⋅
(
η S
η U
)
2
Where the subscripts S and U refer to the standard substance and the test sample, respectively; F represents the integral fluorescence intensity; A represents the absorbance at the excitation wavelength; η denotes the refractive index of the solvent. In order to avoid the interference of the inner filter effect, the absorbances (A) of both standard substance and the test sample should be less than 0.05.

In order to further understand the role of functional groups, various spectral characterizations were used to examine the chemical structure of CDs. Fig. 2 a shows the typical X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns of the three CDs. All of them show broad peaks near 24°, corresponding to graphitic (002) surface, which is consistent with JCPDS Card No. 75–0444, indicating that the three CDs all have graphene structure. Next, Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) was employed to characterize the functional groups of CDs, as shown in Fig. 2b. The peaks at 3500-3300 cm−1 are attributed to the O–H/N–H tensile vibration; the peaks at 1633 cm−1 are the tensile vibration peaks of C = O/C = N; the peaks at 1379 cm−1 correspond the C–N stretching vibration. The peaks at 1037 cm−1 are the stretching vibration absorption peaks of S = O; the peaks at 721 cm−1 are attributed to the S–C tensile vibration absorption. The above results indicate that these three CDs might contain groups such as –NH2, –C = O and S = O. The composition and surface groups of three CDs were characterized by X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). Fig. 2c shows a full survey of XPS of the three CDs, which are composed of C, O, N and S elements. The relative content ratios of C are 64.1%, 75.83% and 73.1% for B-CDs, G-CDs and R-CDs, respectively; the relative content ratios of N are 23.83%, 14.26% and 22.38%, respectively. The content ratio of O changes from 11.54%, 9.31%–3.64%, respectively while the content ratio of S changes from 0.53%, 0.6%–0.79%, respectively, indicating the decreasing of oxygen content while the slight increasing of sulfur content. Fig. 2d–f displays high resolution C1s XPS spectra of the three CDs. One can see each has three characteristic carbon peaks (C–C/C = C) at 284.8 eV, (C–N) at 285.8 eV, and (C = O) at 288.7 eV. The N1s, O1s and S2p high-resolution XPS spectra are provided in the supplementary materials. Figs. S1a–c displays the high-resolution N1s XPS spectra. B-CDs and G-CDs contain two characteristic peaks (C–N–C) at 399.2 eV and (N–H) at 400.2 eV, while R-CDs contain only one characteristic peak (C–N–C) at 399.9 eV. Figs. S1d–e shows the high-resolution O1s XPS spectra of all three carbon dots. All three carbon dots contain two characteristic peaks (S = O) at 532.0 eV and (C = O) at 536.0 eV. Figs. S1g–i shows the high-resolution S2p XPS spectra of all three carbon dots. All three carbon dots contain only one characteristic peak (S = O) at 169.22 eV. From B-CDs, G-CDs to R-CDs, the relative amount of S = O slightly increases. Based on the above results, it can be concluded that all three CDs have graphene structure doped with N and S elements, and there are functional groups such as carbonyl, sulfoxide and amino groups on the surface of CDs.

The UV–vis absorption spectrum, excitation spectrum and the emission spectrum are the basic means to characterize the optical properties of fluorescent materials. Fig. 3 a–c exhibit three-dimensional fluorescence spectra for the blue, green and red CDs respectively, where one can find that they have one, three and two excitation peaks respectively. Fig. 3d–f displays the two-dimensional projections of the three CDs respectively. Their excitation regions are mainly located at 260–460 nm, 250–530 nm, and 300–600 nm while their emission regions are mainly concentrated at 360–600 nm, 460–660 nm and 500–750 nm, respectively. Fig. 3g–i shows the UV–Vis absorption spectrum (purple), excitation spectrum (blue), emission spectrum (multi-color) for each CDs respectively. All the UV–vis absorption spectra have strong absorption bands within 300 nm, which are typical absorption peaks of aromatic benzene rings, originating from the
π → π
*
electronic transition of sp2 hybrid of carbon atoms. In addition, B-CDs display an absorption peak at 345 nm; G-CDs have three at 308 nm, 343 nm and 427 nm, respectively and R-CDs also have three at 323 nm, 374 nm, 517 nm, respectively. These absorption peaks are consistent with the excitation peaks, indicating that these absorption peaks are characteristic absorption peaks, where the absorbed photons may participate in the fluorescence excitation and emission activities. These absorption peaks may be attributed to the
n → π
*
electronic transitions of the functional groups on the surface of CDs, such as –NH–, –SO, –NH2 or other groups. Therefore, these functional groups are likely to be part of the luminescent center. The maximum excitation peaks of the three CDs are located at 372 nm, 439 nm and 517 nm, respectively; the maximum emission wavelengths are located at 439 nm, 528 nm and 623 nm, respectively. For clear observation, these emission spectra are further filled with colors according to the wavelength (Fig. 3g∼i). One can clearly see that the fluorescence emissions of the three CDs are blue, green and red, respectively. Furthermore, the emission spectra show that the full width at half maximum (FWHM) of the three CDs is 85 nm, 77 nm and 110 nm, respectively, indicating that the G-CDs might have better dispersibility than the B-CDs and R-CDs.

In order to study the dependence of the fluorescence emission of CDs on the excitation wavelength, the excitation wavelengths were sequentially changed within the range of 50 nm on both sides of the respective maximum excitation wavelengths, and the changes of the emission spectra of the three CDs were measured, as shown in Fig. 4a-c. For clear observation, these emission spectra are normalized, as shown in Fig. 4 d–f. One can see that G-CDs show an emission behavior independent of excitation wavelength, while B-CDs are significantly red-shifted by the increase of excitation wavelength; the emission of R-CDs is also slightly affected by the change of excitation wavelength, indicating that the photoluminescence mechanisms of the three CDs may be different. CDs have a typical core-shell structure, where the interior is a graphene-structured carbon core covered by groups such as CO, SO, –NH2 on the outer surface. The presence of these heteroatom-containing groups leads to structural defects at the edges of graphene, destroying the conjugated structure of graphene. The luminescence behavior of CDs is closely related to these localized conjugated systems with surface defects. For G-CDs, changing the excitation wavelength, the emission wavelengths almost coincide at 528 nm, indicating that the emission wavelength of the G-CDs does not depend on the excitation wavelength, implying that the surface of G-CDs has a homogeneous chemical state, that is, the types of defects associated with photoluminescence behavior are the same, so the emission wavelength of G-CDs is almost unchanged regardless of the change of the excitation wavelength. In contrast, the fluorescence emission of B-CDs is excitation wavelength dependent, indicating B-CDs might have multiple luminescence centers [32]. R-CDs seem to be between B-CDs and G-CDs. That is, with the chaning of excitation wavelength, and the emission wavelength changes slightly. This result suggests that R-CDs may also have a unique luminescent center, but may be affected by other factors, such as the particle size of the CDs (conjugated effect), etc. Therefore the emission wavelength changes slightly with the excitation wavelength.

In order to study the effect of solvent on the photoluminescence behavior of CDs, H2O (WAT), MeOH (MET), EtOH, Me2CO (ACE), tetrahydrofuran (THF), and N,N -dimethylformamide (DMF) were chosen as solvents to measure the PLQYs of CDs in different solvents. A series of dilute solutions with different concentrations were prepared for each CDs, and their fluorescence spectra and UV–Vis absorption spectra were acquired. The integrated fluorescence intensity was plotted against the UV–vis absorbance, as shown in Fig. 5 a–c. One can estimate the PLQY of CDs using quinine sulfate as the reference. Fig. 5d–f displays the PLQY for the three CDs in different solvents. The PLQY of B-CDs in different solvents is21.3% (H2O), 23.7% (MeOH), 23.0% (EtOH), 23.2% (Me2CO), 5.76% (THF), 28.9% (DMF) respectively. The PLQY of G-CDs in different solvents is 6.32% (H2O), 21.5% (MeOH), 28.3% (EtOH), 33.8% (Me2CO), 30.9% (THF), 31.4% (DMF) respectively. One can see that B-CDs and G-CDs exhibit fully different luminescence performance in different solvents. B-CDs have the highest PLQY in DMF and the lowest one in THF while G-CDs have the highest PLQY in Me2CO and the lowest one in H2O. Similar to G-CDs, R-CDs show highest PLQY in Me2CO, reaching 12.3% while the lowest one in H2O, only 2.07%. From the above results, although B-CDs were synthesized using DMF as solvent, the PLQY only has slight decrease in water. In contrast, both G-CDs and R-CDs were synthesized with ethanol as solvent, and their PLQY decreased seriously in water.

In order to further investigate the optical performance of the three CDs, the effects of solvent polarity, NaCl, pH, irradiation by sun light and so on were examined. Fig. 6 a shows the effect of solvent polarity on the PL intensity of the three CDs. The mixture of EtOH and H2O was served as the solvent, where the polarity could be easily tuned by their volume ratio. When the volume ratio of EtOH and H2O increases (solvent polarity decreases), the PL intensity of R-CDs gradually increases, while the PL intensities of B-CDs and G-CDs both first increase and then decrease. The favorable volume ratios of EtOH and H2O are 60% and 80% for B-CDs and G-CDs, respectively. It can be concluded that the luminescence behavior of CDs is related to solvent polarity [33]. In order to test the PL stability of the three CDs in ionic solution, the effect of different concentrations of NaCl on the fluorescence intensity of the three CDs was investigated, as shown in Fig. 6b. When the concentration of NaCl gradually increased from 0 mM to 200 mM, the PL intensity of the three CDs only changed slightly. This shows that NaCl has little influence on the luminescence properties of CDs and the three CDs keep considerable PL ability even in a high concentration of NaCl solution. Fig. 6c shows the PL response of B-CDs towards pH. When the pH is close to neutral (pH = 6–8) conditions, the B-CDs maintain considerable PL intensity; However, in acidic (pH = 2–6) or alkaline (pH = 8–12) environment, the fluorescence intensity decreased significantly, indicating that the fluorescence quenching of CDs occurred under strong acid or strong base conditions. Then we examined the stability of the three CDs, which were exposed to sunlight to monitor changes in PL intensity, as shown in Fig. 6d. In the case of continuous sunlight irradiation for seven days, no photo bleach was observed for the three CDs, suggesting that the three CDs have good light stability.

Fluorescent CDs have a wide application prospects in many fields, such as display devices, energy conversion, chemical sensing and others. The simplest application is to serve as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting inks, which can be used on ordinary pens, stamps and inkjet printers and so on. The written/printed content is invisible or faintly traced under ordinary daylight, but clearly visible under UV irradiation (365/395 nm). In order to eliminate the aggregation quenching effect, it is better to dilute the stock solution or add fillers before use. For example, using the original solvent or H2O to dilute CDs solution at a ratio of 1:1 may reduce or eliminate the inner filter effect caused by excessive concentration. Fig. 7 displays a traditional Chinese painting drawn in the three-color carbon dots and additional ordinary black ink. The woman's hair and the branches are drawn in black ink, visible in daylight. Other contents are drawn with the CDs, which are not easy to identify in daylight, as shown in Fig. 7a. However, when exposed to UV light, a colorful painting appears. One can see green willow leaves, red lotus flowers, and blue cape on the woman. Even the handwritten poems are clearly identifiable, as shown in Fig. 7b. This is demostrated that the three CDs are free of the classical aggregation-induced quenching effect. Futhermore, as mentioned above, the three CDs are fluorescence emission responsive to pH. For example, their PL emissions may be quenched in a strong acid or alkali environment while can be restored when the pH returns to near neutral, which is a reversible process. This feature allows the CDs to act as UV-pH dual-encrypted fluorescent inks [34].

Using ANSA as the main sources of carbon, nitrogen and sulfur, we successfully synthesized multiple color emissive CDs with blue, green and red colors through different reaction pathways. Their emission wavelengths are 439 nm, 528 nm and 623 nm, respectively, covering short, medium and long wavelengths of visible light. The relative PLQYs of the three multiple-color emissive CDs reach up to 28.9%, 31.4% and 12.3%, respectively, exceeding the PLQY of many similar CDs. Further investigation reveals that the three CDs might have different PL mechanisms.

G-CDs and R-CDs exhibit emission behaviors independent of excitation wavelength, implying both of them might have unique PL emssision centers; while the emission of B-CDs is significantly red-shifted with increasing excitation wavelength, suggesting the emission of B-CDs might be affected by other factors such as conjugated effect. The optical performances of the three CDs are closely related to solvent polarity. The B-CDs possess highest PLQY in DMF while the G-CDs and R-CDs show highest PLQYs in Me2CO. In addition, the B-CDs have good water solubility and response to pH values. The three CDs have excellent photostability and salt resistance. Finally, the use as fluorescent anti-counterfeiting ink technology was taken as an example to briefly introduce the application of multi-color CDs. Actually, due to their broad absorption, long-wavelength excitation, and diverse surface functional groups, the multiple-color emissive CDs also potentially apply to the fields of display device, energy conversion, imaging, chemical analysis. This report synthesized multi-color emissive CDs from same main precursor, which is beneficial to reduce the dependence on raw materials, reduce the cost of large-scale production, and boost their applications.

Xiang Long: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis, paper writing. Chenglin Wang: Sample synthesis, Spectra acquisition, Formal analysis, paper writing, Yanshun Ma: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis, Shaogui Wu: Conceptualization, Methodology, Supervision, Project administration, Formal analysis. Liang Hao: Formal analysis, Result discussion

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This study was supported by Project of Science and Technology Department of Sichuan Province, China (No.2021JDR0131, 21RKX0358).

ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.dyepig.2022.110886.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Quantum-sized carbon dots for bright and colorful photoluminescence

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Single-step synthesis of highly photoluminescent carbon dots for rapid detection of Hg2+ with excellent sensitivity
